quote: Because of obvious reasons, you know, for the economy and for the climate and, we're saving some money.
stewart_read: John offered a genuine, three-part motivation unprompted — economy, climate, savings. The schema flags this as the emotional anchor of the entire pitch and explicitly grades depth: 'Top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Bottom setters check the box and move on.' Carter responded with 'Perfect. Yeah.' and pivoted immediately to verify. The motivation was captured but not deepened — no follow-up on which of the three mattered most, no bill-pain bridge, no curiosity creation off the savings angle.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter may have been banking the motivation to reference later in the call. Rejected — there is no later callback to this moment anywhere in the transcript. The pivot to verify at 00:37 is a clean checkbox move.
coaching_implication: Show Carter the schema depth-grading note on interest_in_solar. Ask: 'What would you have said next if you had 60 seconds here?' The savings angle in particular was a direct bill-pain bridge — 'What are you paying now?' could have flowed naturally and built emotional alignment before the formal verify section.

quote: I'm out of town. I'm in Mexico. So I need to look that up.
stewart_read: John's travel disclosure is a process signal, not an empathy moment. The schema's shopping_customer_protocol is explicit: 'Walk customer through utility website on phone, capture via screenshot text.' Carter acknowledged the travel context for scheduling (time zone negotiation at 03:02–03:36) but never asked the pivotal question: 'Do you have online access to your utility account from your phone?' John said 'I probably could' at 03:46 — hedged, not definitive — which suggests the path was available but untested. Carter accepted the uncertainty rather than solving it.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Carter failed to attempt the shopping_customer_protocol pivot. Strategic read: Carter judged that pushing bill collection on a traveling customer would create friction and chose to defer. Rejected — the strategic read would require Carter to lock the slot first and defer the bill, but Carter did the opposite: deferred both the bill AND the slot, leaving nothing locked.
coaching_implication: Walk Carter through the shopping_customer_protocol. The coaching question is: 'At 01:32, when John said he was in Mexico, what was your next move?' The answer should have been: 'No problem — do you have your utility account on your phone? A lot of people can pull it up right now.' If John can't, then defer to the specialist. But the attempt was never made.

quote: One of the main requirements for getting solar in Colorado is having a credit score above 650. Is that the case for you?
stewart_read: Two issues compound here. First, the schema threshold is 670; Carter stated 650 — a schema drift that has appeared in prior calls (SESSION18_fcbf33c1) and remains unresolved pending Kenny's confirmation. Second, John's response at 02:00 — 'I think yes' — is a soft, unconfirmed qualification. Spencer's operator note in the schema is directly applicable: customers self-report Vantage scores from credit-card apps, not the FICO bureau Ion pulls; a stated 'I think yes' at 650 (or 670) may or may not survive the closer's actual credit pull. Carter accepted the hedge and moved on without probing.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter may have judged that pressing a hedged credit answer would create unnecessary friction on an otherwise warm call. Possible — but the schema's operator note flags that setter DQ on stated credit is a policy question Kenny has not yet resolved. Until that's resolved, the soft confirmation compounds the fragility of the conditional callback.
coaching_implication: Two separate coaching beats here. (1) Confirm with the manager which threshold is active — 650 or 670 — and correct Carter's script. (2) Discuss how to handle a hedged credit answer: the schema's operator note suggests the safer move is to pass through to the closer's pull rather than accept a soft 'I think yes' as qualification. Flag this in the handoff brief so the closer knows credit is unconfirmed.

quote: For about a week, and I'll be traveling. So
stewart_read: John's 'about a week' travel disclosure compounds the no-show risk beyond the Mexico-today context. The schema's soft_outcome_distinction directive is explicit: 'Callbacks WITH locked times vs we'll touch base — major delta on no-show rate.' Carter negotiated a time (9AM John's time / 8AM Carter's time) but then explicitly declined to lock the slot at 04:02, pending bill receipt. The result is a callback promise — not a locked appointment — against a week of travel disruption, with an unconfirmed credit qualification and no bill in hand.
alternative_hypothesis: If this is a paid lead, the schema is explicit that the bill is required before the appointment can be set — meaning Carter's conditional language at 04:02 is schema-compliant procedure, not a failure. The coaching gap in that case is not the bill requirement itself but the absence of conditional slot-locking language: 'I'll hold 9AM your time for you — as soon as the bill comes through, you're confirmed.' That move preserves the slot without violating the paid-lead rule.
coaching_implication: Teach Carter the conditional slot-lock: 'I'll pencil you in for 9AM your time — the moment I get the bill, I'll confirm you.' This costs nothing on a paid lead (slot isn't formally locked until bill arrives) but dramatically reduces the cooling-off risk during a week of travel. The difference between 'I'll text you' and 'I've got your slot held' is the difference between a callback and a sit.

stewart_read: John's hedged 'probably could' on bill access is the second touch of the bill-uncertainty pattern (see multi_touch_patterns). The word 'probably' signals the path is untested, not unavailable. Carter accepted the hedge at face value and moved to the conditional callback. The shopping_customer_protocol pivot — 'Do you have your utility account on your phone? Let's pull it up right now' — was available here and was not attempted.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Carter failed to attempt the pivot. Strategic read: Carter judged that the call was running efficiently and chose to defer rather than risk a 10-minute tech-friction slog. The strategic read has some merit — the schema warns against burning 8–15 minutes on bad paths — but the correct move is to attempt the pivot once, not skip it entirely.
coaching_implication: Pair this moment with the 01:32 entry in the 1-on-1. The pattern is: bill uncertainty surfaced → accepted → deferred → became the gating condition. Carter needs a single-attempt pivot script for traveling customers: 'Do you have the utility app on your phone? Takes 30 seconds.' If John says no, then defer cleanly. But the attempt must be made.

quote: I'm not able to put you in the schedule until I get that. So I'll just shoot you a text.
stewart_read: This line is the call's structural failure point. Carter executes zero button-up elements: no locked time confirmation, no one-hour block stated, no phone-appointment specification, no no-driving/at-computer instruction, no 'is that fair' handshake, no text-followup promise with tieback. The schema treats the button-up as a show-rate protection protocol — each skipped element increases no-show probability. On a conditional callback with a traveling customer, a week of international travel, and an unconfirmed credit qualification, the absence of any anchoring language is the highest-risk outcome of the call.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter may have judged that running a full button-up on an unconfirmed appointment would feel presumptuous and create friction. Rejected — the schema's 'is that fair' handshake and time anchor are explicitly designed for exactly this situation: they create micro-commitments that survive the cooling-off period. The strategic read would require evidence that Carter planned to execute the button-up after bill receipt, which the transcript does not support.
coaching_implication: This is the priority 1 coaching beat. Pull up the button-up checklist with Carter and walk through each element. The specific move to teach: even on a conditional callback, Carter can say 'I've got 9AM your time penciled in — once I get the bill I'll send you a confirmation text with the details. Is that fair?' That single line executes three button-up elements (time anchor, text promise, verbal handshake) in one breath.

quote: And then how much are you paying a month?
stewart_read: Carter captured the bill estimate ($130–$150) and moved on. The schema's bill_anchoring section is explicit: 'capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be FLIPPED into emotional alignment-against-the-utility.' John had already signaled savings as a motivation at 00:34. The flip was available: 'So you're paying $130–$150 a month to Holy Cross right now — that's the number we're trying to eliminate.' Carter did not attempt it. This is the universal coaching gap the schema flags across the entire corpus: 'every rep captured the bill anchor and NONE flipped it.'
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Carter may have been saving the flip for the closer's proposal conversation. Rejected — the setter's job is to create curiosity and emotional alignment before the appointment, not to leave the bill as a neutral data point. The flip at this stage is what makes the appointment feel urgent to John.
coaching_implication: This is the floor-wide coaching gap the schema flags. Use this call as a clean example: Carter had the bill number, had the savings motivation from 00:34, and had a willing customer. The flip was a one-sentence move. Suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'So right now you're sending Holy Cross $130–$150 every month — that's exactly what we're looking to change.' Then move to scheduling. The emotional alignment is set; the appointment has a reason.
